back: A theoretical foundation of RDBMS that provides a set of mathematical operations (Selection, Projection, Union, Difference, Cartesian Product, Rename) to manipulate relations (tables) and retrieve data.

front: Define Selection operation (σ) in Relational Algebra
back: Selection (σ) extracts rows (tuples) that satisfy a given predicate/condition. Example: σsalary>50000(Employee) returns employees earning more than 50000.

front: Define Projection operation (π) in Relational Algebra
back: Projection (π) extracts specific columns (attributes) from a relation, eliminating duplicate tuples. Example: πname,salary(Employee) returns only name and salary columns.

front: What is Union operation (∪) in Relational Algebra?
back: Union combines tuples from two relations having the same set of attributes, removing duplicates. Both relations must be union-compatible (same domains). Example: R ∪ S.
Set Difference returns tuples present in the first relation but not in the second. Example: R − S gives tuples in R but not in S.
Cartesian Product combines every tuple of first relation with every tuple of second relation, creating all possible combinations. If R has n tuples and S has m tuples, R×S has n×m tuples.

front: Define Rename operation (ρ) in Relational Algebra
back: Rename (ρ) is used to rename relations or attributes to avoid naming conflicts. Example: ρEmployeeName/name(Employee) renames the name attribute to EmployeeName.

Natural Join combines two relations based on common attribute names with same values. It performs Cartesian product followed by selection on equal attributes and projection to remove duplicate columns.
Theta Join combines tuples from two relations based on a join condition (θ) which can be any comparison operator (=, <, >, ≤, ≥, ≠). Example: R ⋈ salary>50000 S.
Equijoin is a special case of Theta Join where the join condition uses only equality (=) operator. Example: R ⋈R.id=S.id S joins on equal IDs.
Intersection returns tuples that are present in both relations. R ∩ S is equivalent to R − (R − S). Both relations must be union-compatible.

front: What is Division operation (÷) in Relational Algebra?
back: Division returns tuples from the first relation that match ALL tuples in the second relation. Used for queries like Find students who have taken ALL courses. R(X,Y) ÷ S(Y).

front: What are Outer Joins in Relational Algebra?
back: Outer Joins preserve tuples that would be lost in natural join. Types: LEFT OUTER JOIN (preserves left), RIGHT OUTER JOIN (preserves right), FULL OUTER JOIN (preserves both). Nulls fill missing values.

front: What is the difference between Natural Join and Theta Join?
back: Natural Join joins on all common attributes with same names; Theta Join allows any condition (>, <, =, etc.). Natural Join automatically eliminates duplicate common columns; Theta Join does not.
